content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA 
CHAIRMAN 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
James R. Sutton 
Law Offices of Nielsen, Merksamer 
Parrinello, Mueller & Naylor 
591 Redwood Highway, #4000 
Mill Valley, California 94941 
Re: Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-97-168 
Dear Mr. Sutton: 
August 6, 1997 
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the California Republican 
Party ("CRP") regarding the provisions ofthe Political Refonn Act (the "Act").! 
BACKGROUND 
The California Elections Code designates the California Republican Party ("CRP") as the 
official party organization for the state's Republican party. As such, the CRP maintains a state 
campaign account, registered with the Secretary of State, and a federal campaign account, 
registered with the Federal Election Commission ("FEC"). These accounts finance a range of 
activities, including voter registration and get-out-the-vote drives, educational programs for 
members, development of a platfonn, administration, compliance, fundraising, general office 
overhead, etc. -
as well as direct and indirect support of Republican candidates. 
Several provisions of Proposition 208, particularly the limits on contributions to political 
parties, will impact fundraising by the CRP. You ask a number of questions relating to party 
activities. As you point out, the statutory and regulatory definitions of "contribution" predate 
the enactment of Proposition 208. Therefore, you believe that these provisions provide 
inadequate guidance concerning what types of payments received by the state parties are subject 
to the limits of Sections 85303 and 85310. This maybe true, and amendments to these rules 
(especially Regulations 18215 and 18225) may be justified, but we are reluctant at this stage to 
attempt to anticipate any changes that the Commission or Legislature may wish to make to the 
! Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code ofRegu1ations. 
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 
(916) 322-5660 


File No. A-97-168 
Page 2 
current definition and exceptions from it. We certainly cannot adopt a new exception to 
Regulation 18215 by way of this advice letter. 
Rather, we have applied existing law to your questions, recognizing that this is only the 
beginning and not the end of the discussion that must take place about application of Proposition 
208 to various party activities.2 We realize that there may be many activities of political parties 
that are unrelated to candidate elections in California. We look forward to working with the 
parties and other interested persons to resolve these issues as quickly as possible. 
QUESTIONS 
Are the following types of payments received by the CRP subject to the limits in 
Sections 85303 and 85310? 
(1) 
Payments for the purpose of supporting activities in connection with federal 
elections, including payments to finance lobbying efforts before Congress. 
(2) 
Payments for voter registration and get-out-the-vote drives. 
(3) 
Payments to support or oppose state or local ballot measures, including candidates 
being recalled. 
(4) 
Payments received to finance advertisements regarding public policy issues which 
do not expressly advocate the election or defeat of any candidate ("issue" 
advocacy pieces). 
(5) 
Payments to finance efforts to lobby the State Legislature on matters of 
importance to the Republican platform. 
(6) 
Fees received from members to attend the CRP's semi-annual conventions. 
(7) 
Payments received to finance litigation relating to contractual disputes, personnel 
matters, disputes with members, and challenges to existing laws. 
(8) 
Payments received to finance overhead expenses (e.g., rent, staff salaries, 
supplies, etc.) in connection with member relations, preparing campaign reports, 
fundraising, or research on public policy issues. 
(9) 
Payments received to purchase a computer system to track contributions to the 
CRP and county central committees in order to comply with Section 85303, or 
create a voter file to sell to candidates and third parties, or to prepare campaign 
reports. 
2 We note that the FEe currently is considering petitions asking for rulemaking on these same issues, as 
they apply to the national political parties. 


CONCLUSIONS 
File No. A-97-168 
Page 3 
The limits in Proposition 208 apply to all contributions made to a political party 
committee regardless of how the contributions are earmarked or are later utilized by the party. 
The limits do not apply to "federal" contributions, that is, contributions earmarked for, and used 
in, federal elections. Such contributions are subject to federal law. The limits also do not apply 
to payments that are not deemed to be contributions under state law, such as registration 
payments received for CRP conventions, if the attendees receive full and adequate consideration 
for their fees. 
APPLICABLE LAW 
The Act defines a "contribution" as any payment,3 forgiveness ofloan, a payment of a 
loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent that full 
and adequate consideration is received, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that 
it is not made for political purposes. (Section 82015.) 
Regulation 18215, implementing Section 82015, states in pertinent part: 
"(a) A contribution is any payment made for political purposes for 
which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor. 
A payment is made for political purposes if it is: 
(1) For the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the 
action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of a 
candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any 
measure; or 
(2) Received by or made at the behest of the following or any agent 
thereof" 
(A) A candidate; 
(B) A controlled committee; 
(C) An official committee of a political party, including a state 
central committee, county central committee, assembly district 
committee or any subcommittee of such committee; or 
(D) An organization formed or existing primarily for political 
purposes, including, but not limited to, a political action committee 
established by any membership organization, labor union or 
corporation." (Emphasis added.) 
3 The term "payment" means a payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or other 
rendering of money, property, services or anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible. (Section 82044.) 


File No. A-97-168 
Page 4 
This regulation classifies every payment that a political party receives as a contribution, 
unless a specific exception applies. Underlying this rule is the theory that such political parties, 
like similarly treated candidates and PACs, are inherently "political." As you correctly note, this 
requirement was adopted at a time when full disclosure of contributions was the only objective 
of the regulation. The addition oflimits on contributions to parties may merit a reexamination of 
the rule. 
Section 85303, added by Proposition 208, provides: 
"No person shall give in the aggregate to political party committees of 
the 
same political party, and no such party committees combined shall 
accept from any person, a contribution or contributions totaling 
more than five thousand dollars ($5,000) per calendar year; except 
a candidate may distribute any surplus, residual, or unexpended 
campaign funds to a political party committee." (Emphasis added.) 
Section 85310 states: 
"No person shall contribute in the aggregate more than twenty-
five thousand dollars ($25,000) to all state candidates and the state 
candidates' controlled committees and political party committees 
in any two-year period. Contributions from political parties shall 
be exempt from this provision." (Emphasis added.) 
A "political party committee" is defined in Section 85205 to mean "the state central committee 
or county central committee of an organization that meets the requirements for recognition as a 
political party pursuant to Section 5100 of the Elections Code." 
Consistent with Regulation 18215, we have opined that a payment received by a political 
party, not subject to some exception, is a contribution subject to the contribution limits of 
Section 85303. (Raper Advice Letter, No. I-97-036; Raper Advice Letter, No. I-97-053; 
Bornstein Advice Letter, No. A-97-101.t These same contributions count against the aggregate 
limit in Section 85310. 
4 We have advised that contributions to the state central committee and county central committees of the 
same political party are limited to $5,000 per calendar year. (Section 85311; Bornstein Advice Letter, supra.) The 
Commission will be considering a regulation interpreting Section 85311 in September. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-97-168 
Page 5 
We have reorganized the order of some of your questions to facilitate analysis of the 
issues raised. 
(1) Payments for the purpose of supporting activities in connection with federal 
elections, including payments to finance lobbying efforts before Congress. 
You believe that payments received by a state party for use in federal elections should not 
be regulated by state law. We agree, if certain conditions are met. 
The CRP maintains "federal monies" in a segregated account, subject to federal law. The 
CRP supports candidates running for federal office (e.g., Congress and President). The Federal 
Election Campaign Act (''FECA'') generally controls contributions and expenditures made in 
connection with a federal campaign. (2 U.S.C. § 434, 453.) The FECA provides that the Act 
will "supersede and preempt any provision of State law with respect to election to Federal 
office."s (2 U.S.C. § 453.) The Act does not regulate contributions to federal candidates. (Reed 
Advice Letter, No. 1-88-213; DeYoung Advice Letter, No. A-85-150.) We also have advised 
that the provisions of the Act do not apply to campaign or lobbying efforts which are directly 
solely at federal candidates and issues. (Vergelli Advice Letter, No. 1-93-485.) 
Sections 85303 and 85310 do not expressly limit their scope to payments collected by the 
state party committeesfor state and local races. Nevertheless, in this instance, we conclude that 
such an exception must be implied, since that obviously is the extent of our jurisdiction. 
Therefore, individual payments/contributions to the CRP that are: (1) clearly earmarked in 
writing in their entirety for use in federal elections, issue campaigns, or lobbying; (2) placed in a 
segregated federal account; and (3) not used in California elections, issue advocacy, or lobbying 
are not contributions under state law subject to the contribution limits of Sections 85303 or 
85310. 
(2) Payments for voter registration and get-out-the-vote drives. 
While the FECA's preemption provision is broad, it has been the Commission's view that 
it is not all-encompassing and does not extend to state laws regulating the conduct of state 
elections. (See, e.g., Bovee Advice Letter, No. A-97-172; Litchfield Advice Letters, 
No. A-94-405 and No. A-94-405a.) 
In analyzing voter registration issues relating to state elections, the Commission has 
provided some latitude to organizations who are not "committees" under the Act. For example, 
we have advised that an organization that was not organized for political purposes as defined in 
Regulation 18215(a) did not receive contributions by receiving payments for conducting non-
5 We have not attempted to analyze whether the Federal Election Campaign Act preempts Proposition 208 
in this regard. We urge you to seek an advisory opinion from the FEC if you desire guidance on that issue. 


File No. A-97-168 
Page 6 
partisan voter registration and its communications did not advocate any candidate's election or 
influence voters with respect to the qualification, passage, or defeat of a measure. (Steins Advice 
Letter, No. A-92-102.) 
In In re Welsh (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 78, the Commission concluded that payments to an 
organization which engaged only in non-partisan voter registration, and no other activity in 
connection with elections, did not constitute contributions for purposes of the Act and payments 
by such organizations did not constitute expenditures. The Commission emphasized the non-
partisan nature of the organization conducting the voter registration activities. Under such 
circumstances the organization was not a "committee" and was not required to disclose its 
activities pursuant to the campaign reporting provisions of the Act. 
With respect to contribution limits, the Commission has in the past analyzed Section 
82015 and Regulation 18215 to determine whether contribution limits applied to these efforts. 
The Commission advised that payments to a committee for voter registration were not subj ect to 
the contribution limits of Proposition 73 as long as the activities were not done at the behest of 
candidates. (Calvert Advice Letter, No. A-89-403.) Proposition 73, however, included a 
specific section, Section 85303( c), which exempted from its limits any payments that were used 
for purposes other than making contributions directly to candidates for elective office. 
Proposition 208 does not contain similar language. 
The previous advice noted is distinguishable because the CRP is a "political party 
committee" under the Act, and it is organized for partisan purposes. Therefore, we conclude that 
payments to the CRP for voter registration and get-out-the-vote drives in connection with state 
elections are contributions subject to the limitations of Sections 85303 and 85310. 
(3) Payments to support or oppose state or local ballot measures, including recalls. 
A contribution to the CRP is subject to the contribution limits of Sections 85303 and 
85310, even if it ultimately is used in a ballot measure context. We believe that earmarking a 
payment for a CRP ballot measure account does not change this analysis under Regulation 
18215. On the other hand, when the CRP contributes its funds to a ballot measure committee, 
that expenditure would not be subject to these limits. (See Citizens Against Rent Control v. City 
o/Berkeley (1981) 454 U.S. 290; accord Section 85304; compare Huntley Advice Letter, 
No. A-97-186.) Therefore, contributions to the CRP, even if earmarked for a separate account to 
support or oppose a ballot measure, are subject to the limits of Sections 85303 and 85310. We 
would treat recalls the same.6 
6 The Commission will reexamine later this year the issue of whether candidates and committees involved 
in a recall election should be subject to contribution limits. 


File No. A-97-168 
Page 7 
(4) and (5) Payments received to finance advertisements regarding public policy issues 
which do not expressly advocate the election or defeat of any candidate (issue advocacy 
pieces) and payments to finance efforts to lobby the State Legislature on matters of 
importance to the Republican platform. 
As discussed above, the limitations of Sections 85303 and 85310 are applicable to all 
contributions received by political party committees. No current exemption applies to 
contributions received by a political party committee which are earmarked for, or later used for, 
issue advocacy or lobbying7 the State Legislature. 
(6) Fees receivedfrom members to attend the CRP's semi-annual conventions. 
Section 82015 and Regulation 18215 define a "contribution" to include any payment 
made for political purposes for which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor. 
Ifmembers paying to attend the party's semi-annual conventions are receiving full and adequate 
consideration, no contribution would result. 
(7) Payments received to finance litigation relating to contractual disputes, personnel 
matters, disputes with members, or challenges to existing laws. 
(8) Payments received to finance overhead expenses (e.g., rent, staffsalaries, supplies, 
etc.) in connection with member relations, preparing campaign reports, jundraising, or 
research on public policy issues. 
(9) Payments received to purchase a computer system to track contributions to the CRP 
and county central committees in order to comply with Section 85303, or create a voter 
file to sell to candidates and third parties, or prepare campaign reports. 
Under Section 85303, the limit on contributions a person may make to a political party 
committee is $5,000 per calendar year. Section 85310 establishes a biennial $25,000 aggregate 
limit on any person's contributions to all state candidates and political party committees. The 
contribution limits of the Act apply to both monetary and in-kind contributions of goods and 
services. (Sections 82044 and 82015; Regulation 18215, Bornstein Advice Letter, supra.) 
Therefore, absent an exception, cash payments or in-kind payments such as stafftime or office 
space, or administrative costs of operating a committee made to a committee are contributions. 
(Whipkey Advice Letter, No. A-97-045.) Regulation 18215(c)(2) exempts voluntary personal 
7 It is beyond the scope of this letter to discuss the applicable disclosure requirements under the campaign 
and lobbying provisions of the Act. For example, in the Trier Advice Letter, No. 1-93-398, we advised that pursuant 
to Section 84202.7(b), the special odd-year report was triggered by contributions by a major donor to elected state 
officers even if the contributions have been disclosed on a lobbying disclosure report. We limit our advice to the 
conclusion that payments received by the CRP are subject to the contribution limits of Sections 85303 and 85310. 


File No. A-97-168 
Page 8 
services from the definition of "contribution." Whether this exception applies must be 
determined on a case-by-case basis. 
In addition, in considering whether litigation expenses incurred by a candidate were 
reportable expenditures under the Act, the Commission has distinguished litigation challenging 
the results of an election from litigation challenging the constitutionality or legality of a statute 
enacted by an initiative. (In re Buchanan (1979) 5 FPPC Ops. 14.) However, that opinion was 
interpreting the phrase "[ :t]or the purpose of influencing the action of the voters" in Regulation 
18225 (defining "expenditure"), which is analogous to the language in Regulation 18215 
(defining "contribution"). Unfortunately, that analysis did not address the parts of the 
regulations that classify all payments to and from political parties as contributions and 
expenditures. There appears to be no other applicable exception for the payments received in 
Questions 7, 8, and 9.8 
If you have any questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
Sincerely, 
Steven G. Churchwell 
General Counsel 
c>t£,JC<. fYJo17 (~ 
By: Luisa Menchaca 
Senior Staff Counsel, Legal Division 
8 Regulation 18215(c)(16) applicable to certain overhead expenses paid for by a sponsoring organization 
does not apply to your facts.

thereof"

(B) A controlled committee; 
(C) An official committee of a political party, including a state 
central committee, county central committee, assembly district 
committee or any subcommittee of such committee; or 
(D) An organization formed or existing primarily for political 
purposes, including, but not limited to, a political action committee 
established by any membership organization, labor union or 
corporation." (Emphasis added.)

current definition and exceptions from it. We certainly cannot adopt a new exception to

services. (Sections 82044 and 82015; Regulation 18215, Bornstein Advice Letter, supra.) 
Therefore, absent an exception, cash payments or in-kind payments such as stafftime or office 
space, or administrative costs of operating a committee made to a committee are contributions. 
(Whipkey Advice Letter, No. A-97-045.) Regulation 18215(c)(2) exempts voluntary personal 
7 It is beyond the scope of this letter to discuss the applicable disclosure requirements under the campaign 
and lobbying provisions of the Act. For example, in the Trier Advice Letter, No. 1-93-398, we advised that pursuant 
to Section 84202.7(b), the special odd-year report was triggered by contributions by a major donor to elected state 
officers even if the contributions have been disclosed on a lobbying disclosure report. We limit our advice to the 
conclusion that payments received by the CRP are subject to the contribution limits of Sections 85303 and 85310.  
services from the definition of "contribution." Whether this exception applies must be 
determined on a case-by-case basis. 
In addition, in considering whether litigation expenses incurred by a candidate were 
reportable expenditures under the Act, the Commission has distinguished litigation challenging 
the results of an election from litigation challenging the constitutionality or legality of a statute 
enacted by an initiative. (In re Buchanan (1979) 5 FPPC Ops. 14.) However, that opinion was 
interpreting the phrase "[ :t]or the purpose of influencing the action of the voters" in Regulation 
18225 (defining "expenditure"), which is analogous to the language in Regulation 18215 
(defining "contribution"). Unfortunately, that analysis did not address the parts of the 
regulations that classify all payments to and from political parties as contributions and 
expenditures. There appears to be no other applicable exception for the payments received in 
Questions 7, 8, and 9.8

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are the following types of payments received by the CRP subject to the limits in 
Sections 85303 and 85310? 
(1) 
Payments for the purpose of supporting activities in connection with federal 
elections, including payments to finance lobbying efforts before Congress. 
(2) 
Payments for voter registration and get-out-the-vote drives. 
(3) 
Payments to support or oppose state or local ballot measures, including candidates 
being recalled. 
(4) 
Payments received to finance advertisements regarding public policy issues which 
do not expressly advocate the election or defeat of any candidate ("issue" 
advocacy pieces). 
(5) 
Payments to finance efforts to lobby the State Legislature on matters of 
importance to the Republican platform. 
(6) 
Fees received from members to attend the CRP's semi-annual conventions. 
(7) 
Payments received to finance litigation relating to contractual disputes, personnel 
matters, disputes with members, and challenges to existing laws. 
(8) 
Payments received to finance overhead expenses (e.g., rent, staff salaries, 
supplies, etc.) in connection with member relations, preparing campaign reports, 
fundraising, or research on public policy issues. 
(9) 
Payments received to purchase a computer system to track contributions to the 
CRP and county central committees in order to comply with Section 85303, or 
create a voter file to sell to candidates and third parties, or to prepare campaign 
reports. 
2 We note that the FEe currently is considering petitions asking for rulemaking on these same issues, as 
they apply to the national political parties.

CONCLUSION: The limits in Proposition 208 apply to all contributions made to a political party 
committee regardless of how the contributions are earmarked or are later utilized by the party. 
The limits do not apply to "federal" contributions, that is, contributions earmarked for, and used 
in, federal elections. Such contributions are subject to federal law. The limits also do not apply 
to payments that are not deemed to be contributions under state law, such as registration 
payments received for CRP conventions, if the attendees receive full and adequate consideration 
for their fees. 
APPLICABLE LAW 
The Act defines a "contribution" as any payment,3 forgiveness ofloan, a payment of a 
loan by a third party, or an enforceable promise to make a payment except to the extent that full 
and adequate consideration is received, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that 
it is not made for political purposes. (Section 82015.) 
Regulation 18215, implementing Section 82015, states in pertinent part: 
"(a) A contribution is any payment made for political purposes for 
which full and adequate consideration is not made to the donor. 
A payment is made for political purposes if it is: 
(1) For the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the 
action of the voters for or against the nomination or election of a 
candidate or candidates, or the qualification or passage of any 
measure; or 
(2) Received by or made at the behest of the following or any agent 
thereof" 
(A) A candidate; 
(B) A controlled committee; 
(C) An official committee of a political party, including a state 
central committee, county central committee, assembly district 
committee or any subcommittee of such committee; or 
(D) An organization formed or existing primarily for political 
purposes, including, but not limited to, a political action committee 
established by any membership organization, labor union or 
corporation." (Emphasis added.) 
3 The term "payment" means a payment, distribution, transfer, loan, advance, deposit, gift or other 
rendering of money, property, services or anything else of value, whether tangible or intangible. (Section 82044.)  
This regulation classifies every payment that a political party receives as a contribution, 
unless a specific exception applies. Underlying this rule is the theory that such political parties, 
like similarly treated candidates and PACs, are inherently "political." As you correctly note, this 
requirement was adopted at a time when full disclosure of contributions was the only objective 
of the regulation. The addition oflimits on contributions to parties may merit a reexamination of 
the rule. 
Section 85303, added by Proposition 208, provides: 
"No person shall give in the aggregate to political party committees of 
the 
same political party, and no such party committees combined shall 
accept from any person, a contribution or contributions totaling 
more than five thousand dollars ($5,000) per calendar year; except 
a candidate may distribute any surplus, residual, or unexpended 
campaign funds to a political party committee." (Emphasis added.) 
Section 85310 states: 
"No person shall contribute in the aggregate more than twenty-
five thousand dollars ($25,000) to all state candidates and the state 
candidates' controlled committees and political party committees 
in any two-year period. Contributions from political parties shall 
be exempt from this provision." (Emphasis added.) 
A "political party committee" is defined in Section 85205 to mean "the state central committee 
or county central committee of an organization that meets the requirements for recognition as a 
political party pursuant to Section 5100 of the Elections Code." 
Consistent with Regulation 18215, we have opined that a payment received by a political 
party, not subject to some exception, is a contribution subject to the contribution limits of 
Section 85303. (Raper Advice Letter, No. I-97-036; Raper Advice Letter, No. I-97-053; 
Bornstein Advice Letter, No. A-97-101.t These same contributions count against the aggregate 
limit in Section 85310. 
4 We have advised that contributions to the state central committee and county central committees of the 
same political party are limited to $5,000 per calendar year. (Section 85311; Bornstein Advice Letter, supra.) The 
Commission will be considering a regulation interpreting Section 85311 in September.
embedding.first_500_words: RAVI MEHTA CHAIRMAN FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION James R. Sutton Law Offices of Nielsen, Merksamer Parrinello, Mueller & Naylor 591 Redwood Highway, #4000 Mill Valley, California 94941 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-97-168 Dear Mr. Sutton: August 6, 1997 This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of the California Republican Party ("CRP") regarding the provisions ofthe Political Refonn Act (the "Act").! BACKGROUND The California Elections Code designates the California Republican Party ("CRP") as the official party organization for the state's Republican party. As such, the CRP maintains a state campaign account, registered with the Secretary of State, and a federal campaign account, registered with the Federal Election Commission ("FEC"). These accounts finance a range of activities, including voter registration and get-out-the-vote drives, educational programs for members, development of a platfonn, administration, compliance, fundraising, general office overhead, etc. - as well as direct and indirect support of Republican candidates. Several provisions of Proposition 208, particularly the limits on contributions to political parties, will impact fundraising by the CRP. You ask a number of questions relating to party activities. As you point out, the statutory and regulatory definitions of "contribution" predate the enactment of Proposition 208. Therefore, you believe that these provisions provide inadequate guidance concerning what types of payments received by the state parties are subject to the limits of Sections 85303 and 85310. This maybe true, and amendments to these rules (especially Regulations 18215 and 18225) may be justified, but we are reluctant at this stage to attempt to anticipate any changes that the Commission or Legislature may wish to make to the ! Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18995, of the California Code ofRegu1ations. 428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660 File No. A-97-168 Page 2 current definition and exceptions from it. We certainly cannot adopt a new exception to Regulation 18215 by way of this advice letter. Rather, we have applied existing law to your questions, recognizing that this is only the beginning and not the end of the discussion that must take place about application of Proposition 208 to various party activities.2 We realize that there may be many activities of political parties that are unrelated to candidate elections in California. We look forward to working with the parties and other interested persons to resolve these issues as quickly as possible. (1) Payments for the purpose of supporting activities in connection with federal elections, including payments to finance lobbying efforts before Congress. (2) Payments for voter registration and get-out-the-vote drives. (3) Payments to support or oppose state or local ballot measures, including candidates being recalled. (4) Payments received to finance advertisements regarding public policy issues which do not expressly advocate the election or defeat of any candidate ("issue" advocacy pieces). (5) Payments to finance efforts to
